title: Chapter 44: Scorched Earth
content: Before she went to war, Wren tied up loose ends.

It was raining when she reached Marcus's office — a thin, persistent drizzle that clung to her jacket and flattened her hair against her skull. She stood across the street and watched the building's third-floor window glow with warm light. He was working late. He always worked late.

She fuzzed the lobby camera. Overrode the electronic lock on the service entrance. Took the stairs because the elevator had a camera too, and fuzzing two at once still cost her concentration. The stairwell smelled like industrial cleaner and old coffee — two senses sharp, the cold rail under her hand a third.

Third floor. Marcus's door was closed but the light was on. She could hear him inside — the tap of a keyboard, the creak of his chair, the low murmur of a phone call ending. She waited until she heard him stand, heard his footsteps move toward what she guessed was the bathroom down the hall.
She slipped inside.

His office was exactly as she remembered it. Cluttered desk, overflowing bookshelf, the framed photo of his boat he'd never actually taken out of the marina. A journalism award on the wall — second place, investigative series, six years ago. Marcus had been good once. Before he'd learned that good didn't pay the rent.

She moved quickly. From her jacket she pulled the manila folder she'd prepared — printouts from the Subject 01-13 files, the hard copies she'd made because screens could be scrubbed but paper was harder to erase. She laid them on his desk, fanned out like a hand of cards. The photographs faced up. Petra's crooked smile. Yuki's graduation photo. A boy named David Chen who looked fourteen at most. Thirteen faces. Thirteen erasures.
She placed a note on top. Handwritten, block letters, untraceable: **INVESTIGATE THIS. YOUR CAREER DEPENDS ON IT.**

Not subtle. But Marcus had never responded to subtle. He responded to ambition, to opportunity, to the scent of a story that could make his name. And this story — thirteen people erased by a pharmaceutical company, a drug that rewrote memory, a conspiracy of silence — this was the biggest story of his career. If he was brave enough to chase it.

If he wasn't, the note would end up in his shredder by morning. But Wren was betting on Marcus's vanity. He'd always believed he deserved better than the puff pieces and press releases he'd been reduced to writing. Here was his chance to prove it.
She heard his footsteps in the hall. She slipped behind the door as it opened, pressing herself flat against the wall. Marcus walked in, still drying his hands on a paper towel, and stopped.

She watched his face as he saw the files. Confusion first — the brow furrow, the head tilt. Then curiosity — he picked up the note, read it twice. Then something sharper. His jaw set. His nostrils flared. He picked up Petra's photograph and stared at it.
Wren held her breath. The glitch kept her invisible to cameras, but it didn't make her physically transparent. If Marcus turned around, if he saw her—
He didn't. He sat down. Pulled the files closer. Started reading.
She let herself out.

The rain had worsened by the time she reached her mother's apartment. She stood on the sidewalk and looked up at the fourth-floor window — the one with the blue curtain and the plant on the sill that never seemed to die no matter how irregularly it was watered.

She knew she shouldn't go up. It was weakness. It was risk. Her mother couldn't see her — not because of the glitch, but because Wren didn't exist anymore. There was no daughter to see. The ANX-00 protocol had made sure of that. If Wren walked into that apartment and said "Mom, it's me," her mother would look at her with polite confusion and wonder why a stranger was in her home.
But she had to leave something.
She took the stairs. The building's lobby camera was old and analog — her glitch had no effect on it, but it was aimed at the mailboxes, not the stairwell door. She moved through the gap.

Fourth floor. Apartment 4B. The hallway smelled like her mother's hallway — curry powder from the neighbor at 4A, the floral air freshener her mother insisted on, the faint mustiness of old carpet. Every smell was a wound.

She picked the lock. The deadbolt was new — her mother had changed it after the break-in last spring. But it was electronic, and electronic locks answered to Wren now. She pressed her palm to the reader and felt the mechanism yield.

The apartment was warm. Unbearably warm, the way her mother always kept it, and Wren had to swallow against the tightness in her throat as she stepped inside. The kitchen light was on. A mug of tea sat cooling on the counter. The television murmured from the living room, turned to the news channel her mother watched religiously.

She moved to the kitchen table. From her jacket — the inner pocket, the one closest to her heart — she pulled out the drawing. She'd made it the night before, sitting on the data center floor, using a pen she'd found in a desk drawer and a sheet of printer paper. Two figures: a woman and a girl, holding hands. The woman had long hair and a wide smile. The girl was small, missing a front tooth, wearing a dress with flowers on it.
It was them. From before. From when Wren was seven and the world hadn't yet decided she was a mistake.
She placed the drawing on the kitchen table. Weighed it down with the salt shaker so it wouldn't blow away if the window was open.
Then she heard the footsteps.
Her mother came from the living room, shuffling in slippers, a cardigan pulled over her nightgown. She walked to the kitchen for more tea and stopped when she saw the paper.
Wren pressed herself against the wall beside the refrigerator. Heart hammering. Breath held.

Her mother picked up the drawing. Studied it. Tilted it toward the light. Her brow creased — not with alarm, but with something more complex. Confusion. Nostalgia. A flicker of recognition that didn't have a name because the name had been erased.
She ran her thumb over the girl's face. Then the woman's.
Wren watched from three feet away and felt her chest crack open.

Her mother carried the drawing to the refrigerator. Found a magnet — one of those souvenir magnets from a trip to the coast, faded and peeling. She pinned the drawing to the door. Adjusted it so it was straight.
Then she stood there for a moment, one hand resting on the paper, before she turned off the kitchen light and went back to bed.
Wren didn't move for a long time. The refrigerator hummed. The rain tapped the window. The drawing hung on the door like evidence of a life that someone had tried to delete and failed.
She wished she could have touched her. One touch. One moment of contact that said *I'm here, I'm still here, you didn't lose me, they took me but I'm still here.*
But her mother would have felt nothing. Or worse — would have felt the touch of a stranger and pulled away.
Wren left the way she came. Down the stairs. Out the service entrance. Into the rain.
She didn't look back.

She walked for an hour. Let the rain wash the salt off her face. Let the cold fill the space where the grief had been. By the time she circled back to the industrial district, her jaw was set and her hands were steady and the hollow ache in her chest had hardened into something useful.
Grief was a luxury. She'd indulge it when Ash was safe and Vance was finished and the world knew the truth.
Until then, it was fuel.
She reached the edge of the industrial zone and looked east toward Blackmoss. The sky was dark. The rain was steady. And somewhere out there, in a building that didn't exist, Ash was waiting.
She started walking.
